quote: We just had a a solar design built up for Carol that we weren't able to show her. I was just seeing if there's a better day to get her that information.
stewart_read: Parker's opener is the strongest move on the call — leading with 'a design already built' creates a low-friction reason for the gatekeeper to cooperate rather than screen. This is curiosity framing applied correctly at the gatekeeper layer. Strategic read holds: Parker is not trying to sell solar to the gatekeeper, just open the door to Carol.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Parker stumbled into this framing without intent, and the hesitation ('a a solar design') suggests improvisation rather than a deliberate opener.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this as the correct gatekeeper opener — 'design already built, just need to show her' is a low-resistance frame. Coach Parker to deliver it more cleanly and confidently, without the stutter, so it lands with full weight.

quote: Yeah. To kinda go over to the design or just to get a better time for you? Or
stewart_read: Parker asks the gatekeeper whether the Friday call is to 'go over the design' or 'get a better time' — framing the appointment purpose as ambiguous and deferring to the gatekeeper to define it. This cedes frame ownership at a critical moment. The gatekeeper, not Parker, ends up defining the call's purpose ('you gotta go over the design'). Parker should have owned the frame: the Friday call is to show Carol the design Ion built for her home.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker is probing to understand what the gatekeeper knows about the prior appointment, which could surface useful context. However, the phrasing ('kinda,' 'or') signals uncertainty rather than deliberate probing.
coaching_implication: Coach Parker to own the frame in gatekeeper exchanges. Suggested rephrase intent: 'We have a design ready for Carol — Friday at 3 works great, I'll make sure she has everything she needs to review it.' The gatekeeper doesn't need to define the call's purpose; Parker does.

quote: I believe we have it for an in person appointment. We should be available for an in person appointment at 3PM on Friday.
stewart_read: Parker confirms an in-person appointment format — twice — without any check against Ion's protocol that appointments are phone-based. This is a direct protocol violation. The 'I believe' hedge suggests Parker may not be certain of the format, which makes the double confirmation worse: Parker is locking in a format they're unsure about rather than clarifying.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may be working from a prior appointment record that genuinely specified in-person, and is confirming what's on file. However, even if that's true, the correct move is to convert to phone format per protocol, not confirm the wrong format.
coaching_implication: Pull this clip in the 1-on-1. Ion's appointments are phone-based (button_up.phone_appointment_specified). If a prior record shows in-person, Parker's job is to correct it, not confirm it. The closer will show up expecting a phone call; if Carol expects an in-person visit, the appointment falls apart at contact. Coach Parker to default to phone format and correct any legacy in-person records on the spot.

quote: Okay. Yeah. So, yeah, I had it for an appoint an in person at three on Friday.
stewart_read: This is Parker's second confirmation of the in-person format — now with the gatekeeper after a brief interruption ('Go work. Go work.'). Parker had a natural reset opportunity when the gatekeeper stepped away and returned, but instead re-anchored the wrong format a second time. Two explicit in-person confirmations with the gatekeeper, zero corrections.
coaching_implication: This is a distinct coaching point from the 00:59 moment. The first confirmation could be a slip; the second is a pattern. Coach Parker: every time you say 'in person' on a setter call, that's a flag. The reset after the gatekeeper's interruption was the moment to correct — 'Actually, let me clarify — this will be a phone appointment, so Carol just needs to be available at 3.' That correction takes five seconds and protects the sit.

quote: I'll I'll send you a text or send Carol a text with a confirmation and the date and taxes that she has as well.
stewart_read: Parker promises a confirmation text but directs it ambiguously — 'send you a text or send Carol a text' — without confirming Carol's direct number. The reference to 'taxes' most plausibly refers to tax credits or incentives relevant to the solar design, suggesting Parker intends to include that information in the follow-up. However, the promise is made to the gatekeeper, not Carol, and no button-up elements (verbal handshake, no-driving anchor, computer requirement) were completed with the actual customer.
coaching_implication: The text follow-up protocol (text_after_call) requires a tieback to something specific the customer said. Parker has no direct interaction with Carol to tieback to. Coach Parker: when rescheduling through a gatekeeper, confirm Carol's direct number before closing, and note that the text must go to Carol — not the gatekeeper — with a specific reference to the design built for her home.

quote: Okay. Yeah. We can do that. I'll get that down for, Friday at three.
stewart_read: Parker confirms the time without any verbal handshake ('is that fair?') and without any of the required button-up elements: no one-hour block stated, no phone format confirmed (opposite — in-person confirmed), no no-driving/computer anchor, no 'is that fair' close. The entire button-up protocol is absent. Critically, this is all happening with a gatekeeper — Carol has not been part of any of this confirmation.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may be treating this as a lightweight reschedule callback rather than a full appointment set, and deferring full button-up to the Friday call with Carol directly. However, there is no evidence Parker plans to re-execute button-up on Friday, and the in-person format confirmation suggests Parker believes the appointment is already set.
coaching_implication: Use this call to illustrate what a button-up gap looks like at the gatekeeper layer. The manager should ask Parker: 'What's your plan for Friday — are you calling Carol to confirm, or showing up?' If Parker doesn't have a plan to re-execute button-up with Carol directly before Friday, this appointment is structurally at risk.

quote: Can you call Friday? Because she has a doctor's appointment. This she's in a doctor's appointment this most of this week.
stewart_read: The gatekeeper volunteers that Carol is occupied with medical appointments most of the week — a mild vulnerability signal. Parker's response is purely logistical (proposing 3PM Friday) with no acknowledgment of Carol's situation. This is a low-stakes empathy miss — the gatekeeper is not disclosing a serious condition — but a brief human acknowledgment ('Hope she's doing well — Friday works great') would have warmed the gatekeeper and potentially improved Carol's receptivity when Parker calls Friday.
alternative_hypothesis: The gatekeeper's disclosure is purely scheduling context, not a vulnerability signal requiring empathy. Parker's logistical response is appropriate for the scope of the call.
coaching_implication: Low priority relative to the format and button-up gaps, but worth a brief mention: one sentence of acknowledgment ('Hope she's feeling better by Friday') costs nothing and warms the gatekeeper. Coach Parker to build this reflex — it matters more on calls where the vulnerability is deeper.
